commandPalette.heading: Command Palette
commandPalette.p1: Press <kbd>Ctrl+K</kbd> (or the terminal-prompt icon in the top bar) to open it — type to filter every top-level action (new connection, open/save project, toggle the plotter, Flash/FTP/Plugins/Settings, close/clear the focused tab...) and press <kbd>Enter</kbd> to run the highlighted one, or click any result. Arrow keys move the selection, <kbd>Esc</kbd> closes it.
commandPalette.p2: Every entry that has a keyboard shortcut shows it on the right — this list is generated from the same actions the shortcuts trigger, so it's always accurate even as new panels get added.
commandPalette.p3: Press <kbd>?</kbd> anywhere outside a text field to pop the same shortcut list open directly, without going through the palette or Settings.
commandPalette.p4: A few actions only live in the palette rather than a toolbar button: Search all tabs (Ctrl+Shift+G, searches every open tab's buffer at once instead of just the focused one), Broadcast send (send one command to several open connections at the same time — useful on a production bench), Compare logs (a side-by-side diff of two logs, pasted or loaded from an open tab, with added/removed lines highlighted), and Export diagnostics bundle (a JSON snapshot of the app version, settings, and each tab's recent log for a bug report — passwords/tokens/PINs are redacted first).

layout.heading: Multi-pane Layout (Snap Layouts)
layout.p1: Drag a tab by its label and drop it on the left/right/top/bottom edge of any pane's content area to split the workspace and view two tabs side by side — drop on the edge of an existing pane again to split it further, nesting as many rows/columns as you need. A thin divider appears between panes; drag it to resize.
layout.p2: To merge panes back, drag a tab onto another pane's tab strip (or drop it in the middle of that pane's content area instead of an edge) — the tab moves there, and if that was the last tab in its old pane, the now-empty pane and its split collapse away automatically. Clicking anywhere in a pane focuses it, which is what <kbd>Ctrl+1</kbd>-<kbd>9</kbd>, <kbd>Ctrl+W</kbd>, <kbd>Ctrl+L</kbd>, and <kbd>Space</kbd> act on.
layout.p3: The Plotter is shared across the whole window rather than living inside one pane — toggle it from the top bar and pick its source tab from its own dropdown regardless of which pane that tab is currently showing in.
layout.p4: Each tab shows a small icon for its connection family (serial / network / terminal / SWD), coloured by status — green connected, grey disconnected, red error. Right-click a tab for Rename (give it a meaningful name like "Sensor board" instead of "COM3 · 115200"; it's saved in a project profile), Disconnect/Reconnect, Duplicate (opens a second tab with the same settings), and Close / Close others. Drag a tab left/right past its neighbours in the same strip to reorder it.
layout.p5: The same right-click menu has a color and emoji picker too — pick either (or both) to tell similar-looking tabs apart at a glance; both are saved in a project profile the same as a rename. The clock icon at the left of the top bar (rather than an always-visible list in the New Connection panel) holds Recent Connections — click it, pick an entry, and it reconnects immediately with that connection's settings.

monitor.heading: Serial Monitor
monitor.intro: Click + in the tab strip (or Ctrl+N) to connect. Each connection is its own tab with independent settings. In the monitor toolbar:
monitor.li1: mixed / hex / ascii — switch how each line's bytes are displayed.
monitor.li2: delta / abs / off — timestamp relative to the first line, wall-clock time, or hidden.
monitor.li3: Pause (or Space) freezes the view without losing incoming data — Resume shows how many lines arrived while paused.
monitor.li4: The disk icon starts logging raw + timestamped output to a file, rotated at 50 MB (serial connections only).
monitor.li5: Lines matching common log formats (ESP-IDF's E (328) wifi: ..., [ERROR], or a plain WARN: prefix) are auto-colored by level — no setup needed, this runs alongside filters/search. ANSI color codes in the device's own output (e.g. ESP-IDF/Zephyr's colored log macros) are rendered too, instead of showing up as garbage control characters.
monitor.li6: The bar at the bottom of the monitor shows live bytes/s, lines/s, error count, and uptime for the tab.
monitor.li7: Everything you send (Send panel, quick commands, macros, scripts) is echoed into the log as its own line — marked with a » and shown in the accent color, vs « for lines received from the device — so a request and its reply both show up in the same buffer, in order.
monitor.li8: Right-click a selection (or a line, if nothing's selected) for quick actions: Copy, search the buffer for it, add it as a filter, Inspect bytes (decode it as int8/16/32/64, float32/64, ASCII, and binary, in either endianness), Format JSON/CSV (pretty-print or column-align it in a popup), or — if it looks like a crash backtrace — Decode as crash backtrace, which jumps straight to the Debug tab's crash decoder with the text already pasted in.
monitor.li9: The feature-rail icons on the right also open a Frame builder (compose a binary frame from hex/text/integer fields plus an auto-computed length and CRC, then send it) and a Dashboard (auto-discovers key=value/key: value/flat-JSON fields in the stream and shows the latest value of each as a live widget grid). Next to the Send button, the clock icon repeats whatever's typed there on an interval — a simple heartbeat/poll, no script needed — until clicked again to stop.
monitor.autoReconnect: Enable Auto-reconnect when connecting over serial to have the tab reopen automatically if the device is unplugged and replugged (matched by USB vendor/product id + serial number, not the OS port name, which can change). If the connection drops, use Disconnect/Reconnect in the tab — your COM port, baud rate, and other settings stay filled in.

network.heading: TCP / UDP / WebSocket / MQTT Connections
network.intro: The New connection panel's segmented control isn't limited to Serial — pick TCP Client, TCP Server, UDP, WS Client, WS Server, or MQTT instead, and the tab that opens behaves exactly like a serial one: filters, triggers, macros, the script engine, and the plotter all work unmodified since they only ever see a stream of bytes, not the transport underneath.
network.tcpClient: TCP Client — connect out to a device's IP/port, e.g. a Wi-Fi module exposing a raw TCP console on 192.168.1.50:23.
network.tcpServer: TCP Server — listen on a local port and wait for the device to connect in instead (useful when the device only supports outbound connections). Handles one client at a time.
network.udp: UDP — bind a local port to receive on, and optionally set a remote host/port to send to. Broadcast addresses (e.g. 192.168.1.255) work too.
network.ws: WS Client — connect to a WebSocket endpoint by full URL, e.g. ws://192.168.1.1:81/ (an ESP32 running a WebSocket server is a common target). WS Server listens on a local port instead and waits for a client to connect in, handling one at a time like TCP Server. Incoming text messages appear as one monitor line each; binary messages pass through unmodified. Everything you send — text or hex — goes out as a binary WebSocket frame, so raw/binary protocols round-trip correctly; encrypted wss:// endpoints aren't supported yet.
network.mqtt: MQTT — connect to a broker (host/port, optional username/password), subscribe to a topic filter (e.g. # for everything, or devices/+/telemetry for one level of wildcard), and set a publish topic for the Send panel. Each incoming message appears as a topic: payload line; the client reconnects to the broker on its own if the connection drops, so brief network blips don't need any action from you.
network.mqttExample: Example — watch and command an MQTT device: broker broker.hivemq.com:1883, subscribe topic devices/kitchen-sensor/#, publish topic devices/kitchen-sensor/cmd. Incoming readings show up as devices/kitchen-sensor/temp: 24.5; typing reboot in the send box publishes it straight to the cmd topic.
network.findingDevices: Finding devices: for TCP Client, WS Client, and MQTT targets, the Scan LAN button below the connection fields browses the local network via mDNS/DNS-SD for a chosen service type (HTTP, MQTT, Arduino OTA, ESPHome, WebSocket) and lists what it finds — click a result to fill in the host and port automatically. Devices must advertise themselves via mDNS (most IoT firmware frameworks do) and be on the same subnet.

ssh.heading: SSH Terminal
ssh.p1: Pick SSH in the New connection panel and enter host, port, username, and password. Unlike the other connection kinds, an SSH tab opens a real interactive terminal (a PTY, rendered with an actual terminal emulator) instead of a line-oriented monitor — there's no raw-log toggle, no Send panel, and no filters/triggers/script engine, since those all assume a stream of discrete lines rather than a live shell. The terminal resizes itself (and tells the remote shell) whenever its pane changes size, including when you split or merge panes.
ssh.p2: While an SSH tab is focused, <kbd>Ctrl+W</kbd> and <kbd>Ctrl+L</kbd> go to the shell (delete word / clear screen) instead of being caught as the app's close-tab / clear-log shortcuts. Paste with <kbd>Ctrl+V</kbd> or right-click — both read from the system clipboard directly rather than relying on the browser's native paste, which isn't reliable inside the app's webview. Selecting text copies it automatically (no keyboard shortcut needed, since <kbd>Ctrl+C</kbd> sends SIGINT to the remote shell instead) — double-click selects a word, triple-click a whole line.
ssh.p3: A terminal has no concept of "click to move the cursor mid-command" the way a text editor does — the remote shell (not this app) owns the command line you're typing, so editing it works the same as any other terminal: <kbd>Home</kbd>/<kbd>End</kbd>, <kbd>Ctrl+←</kbd>/<kbd>Ctrl+→</kbd> to jump by word, and <kbd>Ctrl+A</kbd>/<kbd>Ctrl+E</kbd> to jump to the start/end of the line.
ssh.p4: Two current limitations worth knowing: authentication is password-only (no key-based auth yet), and the host key isn't verified against a known-hosts list — any server's key is accepted, so this isn't yet safe against an on-path attacker impersonating the host.
ssh.p5: If a connection drops, the tab shows an inline password field and Reconnect button right there — no need to close the tab and reconnect from scratch via New Connection. Tick Remember next to it to save that password in the OS's own credential store (Windows Credential Manager / macOS Keychain / Linux Secret Service) so it's offered again automatically after an app restart; it's opt-in and off by default.

modbus.heading: RS485 & Modbus RTU
modbus.rs485: For RS485 transceivers whose DE/RE direction pin has no auto-direction circuitry (common on bare MAX485-style breakout boards, unlike most all-in-one USB-RS485 dongles), check RS485 half-duplex (auto RTS toggle) when connecting over Serial. RTS is then asserted right before every write and held until the bytes have actually finished shifting out at the configured baud rate, then released — timed from the baud rate and frame size, not a fixed guess, so the last byte doesn't get clipped. The manual RTS button in the signal bar is disabled while this is on, since toggling it by hand would fight the automatic control.
modbus.toolsIntro: Two sidebar tools speak Modbus on top of a connection:
modbus.master: Modbus Master (gauge icon) — build a one-shot request (slave address, function code, start address, quantity or value, timeout) against a real Modbus RTU device and see the parsed response, or set up poll rules to read a register repeatedly on an interval — each rule's value also feeds the Plotter automatically, using the rule's label as the channel name. Only one request is ever in flight on the bus at a time, whether it came from a manual click or a poll rule, since RS485 is half-duplex. The Master also works on a TCP Client tab: connect to a Modbus TCP device or gateway (port 502 by convention) and the same requests go out MBAP-framed as Modbus TCP — the "Slave address" field becomes the Unit ID. The Slave emulator remains serial-only.
modbus.slave: Modbus Slave (chip icon) — emulate a Modbus RTU device to test a real master against. Set a slave address, fill in whichever coils/discrete inputs/holding registers/input registers you want it to answer for, click Start listening, and it responds to matching requests automatically — including a spec-correct exception response if the master asks for a register you haven't added. It keeps listening in the background even if you switch to a different sidebar panel or tab. A tab can't run both Master polling and Slave listening at once — enabling one disables the other, since one RS485 bus can't sensibly be both roles at the same time.
modbus.example: Example — read holding register 0 from slave address 1: in Modbus Master, set slave address 1, function 03 Read Holding Registers, start address 0, quantity 1, then Send. On the wire this is the request 01 03 00 00 00 01 84 0A (the trailing two bytes are the CRC16). You can see this exact codec in action without any hardware at all: open Modbus Slave on a second tab connected to the other end of a loopback/null-modem pair, add holding register 0 with some value, enable Start listening, and send the request from the first tab's Master panel to watch the round trip end to end.

presets.heading: Saved Profiles, Scripts & Presets
presets.p1: Four places share the same small save/load control — a dropdown plus a disk icon (save) and trash icon (delete): the Profile picker in the New connection panel, Script in the script panel, and Preset in both Filters and Triggers. Picking an item from the dropdown loads it immediately; the disk icon prompts for a name and saves whatever is currently filled in (overwriting if you reuse a name); the trash icon deletes whichever item is selected. Everything is stored locally in the app, so it's there next time you open EDT — nothing to export/import manually.
presets.p2: Example: set up a TCP Client profile for your dev board once, save it as dev-board, and pick it from the Profile dropdown on any future connection instead of retyping the host/port. Same idea for a Lua script you use across multiple tabs, or a filter preset like ERROR|WARN + exclude heartbeat that you'd otherwise rebuild by hand each time.

projectProfiles.heading: Project Profiles
projectProfiles.p1: The folder icon in the top bar (Open project) and the disk icon next to it (Save project) save/restore the whole workspace to a .edtproj file on disk — every open tab's connection, its filters/triggers/script, the Snap Layout arrangement, and the Plotter's config (source tab, extractors, math channels, thresholds, chart type — not the plotted data itself, which repopulates once the source tab reconnects). Unlike the Profile/Script/Preset pickers above, this is a real file you can move between machines or hand to a teammate working on the same device, not something tucked away in local app storage.
projectProfiles.p2: Opening a project reconnects every saved connection under a new tab and closes whatever was open before — you'll be asked to confirm if you have tabs open already. SSH connections pause for a password prompt each time (passwords are never written to the file); a connection that fails or whose password prompt you cancel is simply left out of the restored layout rather than blocking the rest.

search.heading: Search & Bookmarks
search.p1: Press <kbd>Ctrl+F</kbd> to open an in-buffer regex search bar. <kbd>Enter</kbd> / <kbd>Shift+Enter</kbd> jump to the next/previous match; <kbd>Escape</kbd> closes it. Search runs over whatever filters currently have applied, so you can narrow down with a filter first and search within that subset.
search.p2: Click the bookmark icon on any line (or use a Bookmark line trigger action, see Triggers below) to mark it. The bookmark arrows in the toolbar jump between marked lines in order — handy for flagging a handful of interesting moments in a long capture and revisiting them without re-reading everything in between.
search.p3: To search across every open tab at once instead of just this one, use <kbd>Ctrl+Shift+G</kbd> (see Command Palette above).

sendMacro.heading: Send Panel & Macro Recorder
sendMacro.p1: Type in the send box and press Enter, or switch to hex mode to send raw bytes like 01 02 FF. Pick the line ending (None/CR/LF/CRLF) once per tab. Arrow up/down recalls previous sends.
sendMacro.p2: The second dropdown next to Line Ending is Checksum — pick CRC16 (Modbus), CRC16 (CCITT), CRC8, XOR, or Sum, and it's appended to every message you send from this tab, in both text and hex mode (a Modbus RTU frame typed as hex, for instance, gets the correct CRC16 tacked on automatically). Turning a checksum on also suppresses the line ending for that send — a trailing CR/LF after a binary checksum would corrupt the frame — so there's nothing else to configure for binary protocols.
sendMacro.p3: The macro recorder lives in the sidebar next to Filters/Triggers/Script — click the repeat icon to open it.
sendMacro.exampleLabel: Example — record and replay a test sequence:
sendMacro.step2: Send AT from the send panel, wait a moment, then send AT+GMR.
sendMacro.step4: Click Play any time to replay both commands with the same real delay between them, or edit a step's delay / remove it right there in the panel.

quickCommands.heading: Quick Commands
quickCommands.p1: The slim row above the send box is for one-click, frequently-reused commands (think AT commands, a reboot string, a status query) — unlike the macro recorder, there's no sequence or delay involved, each chip just fires that one command immediately when clicked.
quickCommands.p2: Commands are grouped into named profiles (the dropdown on the left) so you can keep separate sets for different devices — saved permanently, and shared by every tab, not just the one you created them in. Pick a profile to show its commands; the + button next to the dropdown creates a new one.
quickCommands.li1: + Add at the end of the row opens a small form below for the command's label (optional — falls back to showing the command text itself), the text or hex payload to send, and a hex toggle.
quickCommands.li2: Click the pencil on any chip to edit it, or the × to remove it.
quickCommands.li3: Drag a chip and drop it on another to reorder — the order is the priority, and is saved back to the profile immediately.
quickCommands.li4: Tick JSON (next to Hex) when writing a JSON payload — the single-line field switches to a multi-line box so indentation is readable, plus a Format button. It's compacted back to one line automatically when saved, so what's actually sent stays a single write (embedded newlines would otherwise split it for any device reading line-by-line).
quickCommands.li5: The JSON box behaves a bit like a code editor: Tab inserts an indent instead of jumping to the next field (Shift+Tab removes one), Enter auto-indents to match the current nesting (and expands {}/[] onto three lines when pressed right between an empty pair), and typing {, [, or " auto-inserts the matching closer with the cursor placed in between.
quickCommands.li6: The line ending dropdown next to Hex/JSON lets one command override the tab's Line Ending setting (e.g. force CRLF for a command that needs it regardless of what the monitor is currently set to) — leave it on "tab default" to keep following the tab's setting as before.

filters.heading: Filters
filters.p1: Click Filters in the monitor toolbar. Each rule is a regex, either include (only show matching lines) or exclude (hide matching lines). Rules stack, matches are highlighted, and each rule shows a live count.
filters.example: Example: add an include rule ERROR|WARN to only see errors/warnings, plus an exclude rule heartbeat to additionally hide a noisy keep-alive line.

triggers.heading: Triggers
triggers.p1: Click Triggers to react automatically when a line matches a pattern — no code needed. Each rule is a regex plus one action:
triggers.li1: Send — pattern READY → send AT+INFO\r\n to auto-query the device right after it boots.
triggers.li2: Play sound — pattern ERROR → get an audible alert without watching the screen.
triggers.li3: Write to file — pattern FAIL → append every failing line to its own log file.
triggers.li4: Bookmark line — pattern ERROR → mark every error line so you can jump between them later with the bookmark navigator.

script.heading: Script Engine (Lua)
script.intro: Click Script to write a small Lua program for that tab. Available functions:
script.li1: on_data(line) — define this function and it's called once per received line.
script.li3: wait_for(pattern, timeout_ms) — blocks until a matching line arrives or the timeout elapses; returns the matched line or nil.
script.li5: plot(channel, value) — push a point straight into the plotter, if this tab is its selected source.
script.li6: timer(interval_ms, fn) — run fn repeatedly on a schedule.
script.example1Label: Example — extract a value and auto-reply once ready:
script.sandboxNote: This is a convenience sandbox (no os/io/require, each callback capped at ~2s) for scripts you write yourself — not a hardened boundary against malicious code.

plugins.heading: Plugins
plugins.intro: A plugin is a named, reusable Lua file — unlike a script (one free-form slot per tab), install a plugin once from the Plugins button in the top bar, then enable it on any tab from that tab's own Plugins button; several plugins can run on the same tab at once. A plugin defines exactly one pure function and has no send/wait_for/timer — it only describes how to read data, never drives the device.
plugins.manifest: The manifest is just leading -- key: value comment lines at the top of the file — name (required), kind (required, decoder or plotter-parser), and optional version/description. No separate manifest file, so installing a plugin is picking one .lua file.
plugins.installUrl: Next to the file picker, a URL field installs a plugin straight from a link to a raw .lua file instead of downloading it yourself first.
plugins.decoderLabel: A decoder plugin defines decode(line), returning a table of fields shown live in that tab's Plugins panel:
plugins.plotterLabel: A plotter parser plugin defines parse(line), returning { channel = number, ... } pairs fed straight into the plotter, the same as a script's plot(...):
plugins.attachNote: A plugin's own errors (bad pattern, wrong return type) show inline next to its checkbox in the Plugins panel rather than stopping the whole tab — one bad line doesn't take the plugin down.

plotter.heading: Plotter
plotter.p1: Toggle the chart icon in the top bar (Ctrl+Shift+P) and pick a source tab. Lines are auto-parsed as CSV (1.2,3.4), space-separated, or key:value pairs (e.g. temp:24.5,hum:51.2) — up to 8 channels. Each channel keeps a sliding window of the most recent points, dropping the oldest once full; Settings → Plotter buffer size controls how many (1,000–500,000, default 50,000) — raise it if you're watching a long-running session and don't want early data pushed out.
plotter.p2: Example — extract a value that isn't in a recognized format: a log line like Current temperature reading: 24.5C won't auto-parse. Open Extractors and add pattern temp=(\d+\.\d+) → channel temp (adjust the pattern to match your actual log text) to pull the number out anyway. A script's plot(...) calls land on the same chart too.
plotter.toolsIntro: The toolbar also has a set of analysis tools:
plotter.math: Math — derived channels computed from real ones: A+B, A−B, A×B, A÷B, moving average, derivative (units/second), or RMS over a sample window. They show up in the legend prefixed with ƒ and behave like normal channels everywhere — stats, FFT, CSV export.
plotter.levels: Levels — horizontal alert lines. Pick a channel and a value; a dashed line is drawn at that level and a beep sounds whenever the channel crosses it upward. Example: channel temp > 80 beeps the moment a temperature reading exceeds 80.
plotter.stats: Stats — a live strip showing each visible channel's min / max / avg / peak-to-peak and an estimated frequency (blank for flat or non-periodic signals).
plotter.measure: Measure — click two points on the chart to read the exact Δt (and its 1/Δt frequency) and each channel's Δy between them, like an oscilloscope's cursors. Turning it on freezes the chart so the markers stay put; click a third point to start a new measurement, and turn Measure off (then Freeze off) to resume live plotting.
plotter.colors: Channel colours — the swatch next to each channel in the legend is a colour picker; click it to recolour that channel. The choice sticks to the channel by name and is saved in a project profile. The checkbox beside it hides/shows the channel without deleting its data.
plotter.fft: FFT — switches the chart to the frequency domain: the current buffer is resampled to a uniform rate, a window function (Hann by default, Hamming or rectangular selectable) is applied, and the amplitude spectrum is shown with Hz on the x axis. Needs at least 64 samples. Note: values between real samples are held constant by the plotter's ingest (a zero-order hold), which adds small artificial harmonics — fine for spotting dominant frequencies, not for precision measurement.
plotter.csvPng: CSV / PNG — export the whole buffer (timestamps + every channel including math channels) as CSV, or the chart as a PNG image (the HTML legend below the chart isn't part of the image).

flashEsp32.heading: Flash ESP32
flashEsp32.step1: Open the flash panel (zap icon or Ctrl+Shift+F), keep ESP32 selected.
flashEsp32.step2: Pick the port and baud rate, then Detect chip.
flashEsp32.step3: Add segments manually — Example: offset 0x1000 for the bootloader, 0x8000 for the partition table, 0x10000 for the app binary (offsets vary by project — check your build output) — or use Smart add (below) to fill these in automatically.
flashEsp32.step4: Click Flash; Erase chip wipes everything first if needed.
flashEsp32.smartAdd: Smart add lets you pick your build output files (bootloader, partitions, firmware, a filesystem image) all at once, and fills in the right offset for each — reading the actual compiled partition table when you include one (partitions.bin/partition-table.bin) rather than guessing, since a filesystem image's offset shifts with flash size and partition scheme and a wrong guess could overwrite the app. The bootloader offset is filled in from the detected chip (0x1000 on original ESP32, 0x0 on S2/S3/C2/C3/C6/H2 — run Detect chip first for this to be exact). If a selected partition table needs an OTA "boot selector" (otadata) image and you didn't include your own boot_app0.bin, EDT supplies its own bundled copy automatically. Anything Smart add can't confidently match is added with a blank offset rather than a guess — fill those in by hand.
flashEsp32.batch: Batch (next to Single) flashes the same segments to several boards at once — check every port to include, then Flash N device(s). Each port flashes on its own, in parallel, with its own live progress/status, so one board failing (or being unplugged) doesn't stop or block the others.
flashEsp32.autoFlash: Auto-flash on plug (in Batch mode) skips the checkbox step entirely: once armed, any newly plugged device that looks like an ESP32 (by its USB serial chip — CP210x, CH340, FTDI, or Espressif's native USB) is flashed immediately with the current baud rate and segments, no per-device confirmation. Meant for repetitive production flashing — plug the next board, it flashes itself. It skips a port that's already open in another tab, and the VID/PID check is only a pre-filter: flashing a non-ESP32 device fails harmlessly at the chip handshake rather than writing anything. Leave it off unless you're actively flashing a batch — it stays armed for as long as the Flash panel is open.
flashEsp32.provision: Provision (third mode, next to Batch) is separate from flashing — it sends a scripted sequence of plain serial commands to an already-flashed device instead of writing firmware. Build a list of steps (each one a command, a line ending, and either "wait for response" with a match string/timeout or a fixed delay), pick a baud rate, then either Run now on a currently-plugged port or arm Auto-run on plug so the workflow fires by itself the moment a matching device is connected — handy for commissioning many boards with the same WiFi credentials/device ID/config commands in a row. Steps and baud rate are remembered across restarts; arming is not, for the same reason auto-flash isn't remembered either.
flashEsp32.security: A fourth mode, Security, reads a curated set of security-relevant eFuses (flash encryption counter, secure boot enable, JTAG/UART-download disable) straight from the chip — no Python/espefuse.py needed. Burning is irreversible and only the fields the chip's own eFuse layout returns can be burned; read the in-panel warning and Espressif's flash encryption / secure boot docs before using it, since a bit burned without also completing the required key setup can leave a device permanently unbootable.

ota.heading: OTA Update (WiFi)
ota.p1: The OTA (WiFi) target in the flash panel flashes a device over WiFi using the ArduinoOTA protocol — no USB cable, no serial port. The device must already be running firmware built with ArduinoOTA.h (any Arduino/PlatformIO Arduino-framework sketch that calls ArduinoOTA.begin()); this doesn't work against a device that's never been flashed with OTA support at least once over USB first.
ota.p2: Scan for OTA devices browses the LAN via mDNS for the _arduino._tcp service (the same discovery ArduinoOTA itself advertises, and what the Arduino IDE's Network Ports menu uses) — click a result to fill in host and port. Otherwise enter the device's IP manually (port defaults to 3232). Leave the password field blank if the sketch never called ArduinoOTA.setPassword(). Progress goes through an invite/authenticate handshake before any bytes transfer, then a single continuous upload — there's no per-segment offset to configure, the device's own OTA partition handling takes care of that.
ota.toastNote: A toast notification in the bottom-right corner reports success/failure when this (or a batch flash, auto-flash-on-plug, or provisioning run) finishes, whether or not this panel is still open — you don't have to keep watching it.

debug.heading: Debug (memory map + crash decoder)
debug.p1: A fourth flash-panel target that reads a build's .elf file directly — the same one a build already produces, no separate .map file and no bundled toolchain binary (like xtensa-esp32-elf-addr2line) needed. Works the same for Xtensa or RISC-V ESP32 variants, or STM32/ARM, since DWARF debug info and ELF section headers aren't architecture-specific.
debug.p2: Memory map — pick the .elf and click Analyze to see flash/RAM usage broken down by section, sorted largest first. Flash bytes are text+rodata+data (anything written into the binary); RAM bytes are data+bss (anything resident at runtime) — classified from the section header's actual type/flags, so it stays accurate even for ESP-IDF's non-standard section names like .iram0.text.
debug.p3: Crash decoder — paste a device's crash output (e.g. a Backtrace: 0x400d1e2d:0x3ffb9f30 ... line from a Guru Meditation Error) and click Decode to resolve each address to a function name and file:line, using the same .elf's debug info. Only the PC half of each address pair is resolved (the SP half isn't a code address); addresses with no matching debug info still show up, just without a function/location. Click a resolved file:line to open it directly in VS Code (via the code CLI).
debug.p4: Core dump decoder — paste the base64 blob a device prints on panic (ESP-IDF's CONFIG_ESP_COREDUMP_ENABLE_TO_UART), with or without the CORE DUMP START/END markers, and click Decode core dump. It's decoded and scanned for values that look like code addresses, each resolved against the same .elf as the crash decoder above. This is a heuristic scan rather than a full unwinder, so expect some noise alongside the real frames — treat it as a starting point, not a definitive stack trace.

ftp.heading: FTP Client/Server
ftp.p1: The Client tab connects to any FTP server — an SD-card or SPIFFS/LittleFS file browser many ESP32/ESP8266 sketches expose, a NAS, or another computer — and lets you browse folders, upload, download, create folders, and delete files. Enter host/port/username/password and hit Connect; leave username as anonymous with a blank password for servers that don't require login. Only plain FTP is supported (no FTPS/TLS), which covers the vast majority of embedded-device FTP servers.
ftp.p2: The Server tab runs a local FTP server so a device can read/write files on this computer instead — pick a root folder to share, a port, and optionally a username/password (leave username blank for anonymous access). Stop the server before changing its settings. Like the client, this is plain FTP only.
ftp.note: Both sides handle one connection at a time for now — this is a file browser, not a byte-stream tab, so it doesn't appear in the tab strip alongside Serial/TCP/SSH.

flashStm32.heading: Flash STM32
flashStm32.intro: Requires STM32CubeProgrammer installed — the panel detects it automatically.
flashStm32.stLink: ST-Link (SWD) — for boards with an on-board or external ST-Link.
flashStm32.uart: UART bootloader — for boards without one; put the MCU in bootloader mode via BOOT0 first.
flashStm32.optionBytes: Option-byte writes (e.g. RDP level) require an explicit confirmation dialog — read it before confirming, as some settings can permanently affect debug access.
flashStm32.security: The Security section gives the three standard Readout Protection levels clear explanations instead of raw option-byte names: Level 0 (no protection), Level 1 (reversible — revert by mass-erasing back to Level 0), and Level 2 (permanent — cannot be undone, ever). Level 2 requires typing LOCK to confirm, on top of the usual dialog. The raw name/value option-byte editor below it still exists for fields Security doesn't curate (e.g. per-family Write Protection), since field names vary too much between STM32 families to curate safely.
flashStm32.massProduction: A second mode next to Single, Mass Production, flashes the same firmware to many boards in a row — pick a byte offset/length in the binary and a value format (zero-padded ASCII decimal, or raw hex bytes), and each device gets an auto-incrementing counter patched in before it's flashed (a copy is patched, the original file is never touched). Flash & Provision does one device at a time: plug a board, click it, wait, unplug, plug the next, repeat. Every attempt is logged (value, pass/fail) with a CSV export, and also feeds the Stats tab's totals.
flashStm32.pathAndAddress: The firmware-file field accepts a typed/pasted path in addition to Browse. Selecting a .hex file auto-fills the address from the file's own embedded records (a .bin carries no such information, so that one still needs the address typed in). STM32_Programmer_CLI also runs without popping up its own console window now.
flashStm32.writeMemory: The Write memory section (below Option bytes) writes arbitrary content — text, hex bytes, a decimal integer, or JSON — to a single address, independent of the firmware file above: pick a content type and an address, then Write. The content is staged into a small temp file and flashed the same way a full firmware image would be.

flashLock.heading: Flash Lock (PIN)
flashLock.p1: Off by default — turn it on in Settings and set a PIN. Once on, every flash action (ESP32/STM32 single flash, batch flash, mass production) prompts for that PIN first; entering the wrong one (or cancelling) aborts the flash. This is meant to stop a shared production station from being flashed by whoever walks up to it, not to withstand a determined attacker — the PIN is stored in plain text on this machine, the same way the REST API token is.

swd.heading: SWD Debug (RTT + variable watch)
swd.p1: A SWD connection (next to SSH in the New Connection picker) attaches to a debug probe (ST-Link, J-Link, CMSIS-DAP...) instead of a serial port — pick the probe (or leave it on Auto) and type the target chip name (e.g. STM32F407VG; suggestions come from probe-rs's built-in chip database as you type). It opens as a normal Monitor tab.
swd.p2: RTT log streams straight into that tab's log the same way a serial port would — but only if the firmware actually initializes an RTT control block (e.g. the rtt-target crate, or SEGGER RTT in C). It's not automatic like UART; if the firmware doesn't have it, the tab still opens fine, it just won't show log lines.
swd.p3: Watch (below the log, replacing the Send box — RTT down-channel writes aren't supported) reads global/static variables live over the same SWD session. Load the build's .elf, pick a variable from the list (parsed from its DWARF debug info), and its value refreshes continuously while the chip runs — no need to halt at a breakpoint. Only global/static variables are listed; local variables inside a function need a real halt-and-inspect debugger, which this isn't.

restApi.heading: Local REST API
restApi.p1: Off by default — enable it in Settings, where you'll also set its port and find its bearer token. Once on, it binds to 127.0.0.1 only (never the LAN) and lets an external CI/production-line system open a serial port, write and read bytes, list ports, and trigger an ESP32 flash on this running instance — the same manager state the GUI itself uses, so a port opened this way shows up as open in the app too. Every request needs Authorization: Bearer <token>.
restApi.p2: GET /api/v1/status and /serial/ports (available) / /serial/open (currently open) don't need a body. POST /serial/open, /serial/write (hex-encoded bytes), /serial/read (blocks up to timeoutMs for the next batch of data), /serial/close, and /esp32/flash (blocks until the flash finishes, returns pass/fail) take a JSON body:

productionStats.heading: Production Stats
productionStats.p1: A fifth flash-panel target totaling every device flashed by a production workflow — ESP32 Batch mode (including auto-flash-on-plug) and STM32 Mass Production — across every session, not just the current one. Shows total/success-rate/last-24h/last-7-days counters plus a recent-activity table, with a CSV export and a way to clear the whole history. Ad-hoc single-device flashes during development aren't counted, so the numbers stay meaningful for a production report.

configBackup.heading: Backup & Restore Configuration
configBackup.p1: In Settings, Export configuration bundles every saved library — connection profiles, the script library, installed plugins, quick-command profiles, and filter/trigger presets — into one JSON file, for backing this machine up or handing a teammate the same setup. Import configuration reads one back in; each item is upserted by name (a same-named local item is overwritten, not duplicated). Personal preferences (theme, language, the REST API token) and auto-tracked history (recent connections, notifications, production stats) are deliberately left out — those aren't things you'd want to overwrite on someone else's machine.

app.confirmCloseAllTabs: Opening a project closes every tab currently open. Continue?
app.partialRestoreWarning: {{failed}} of {{total}} connection(s) could not be reopened.

ssh.rememberPasswordHint: Saves this password in your OS credential store (Windows Credential Manager / macOS Keychain / Linux Secret Service) so it survives an app restart.
ssh.forgetHostKeyHint: Only do this if you're sure the host's key legitimately changed (e.g. the server was reinstalled) — this clears the saved fingerprint so the next connection is trusted again without asking.

sftp.noFileOpen: No file open. Browse the sidebar and click a file to edit it.
sftp.binaryFile: This file looks binary and can't be edited here.
sftp.largeFileConfirm: "{{name}}" is {{size}} — opening it may be slow. Open anyway?
sftp.closeTabUnsavedConfirm: This SSH tab has unsaved file changes. Close anyway and discard them?
sftp.connectionLostWhileEditing: SFTP connection lost — unsaved changes can't be saved until you reconnect.

connect.title: New connection
connect.rs485Title: Toggles RTS around each write for RS485 transceivers whose DE/RE pin has no auto-direction circuitry
connect.sshPassphraseOptional: Leave blank if the key isn't encrypted
connect.onboardingHint: New here? Press Ctrl+K anytime to search every action, or open the User Guide from Settings for a full walkthrough.
connect.noProbesFound: No debug probes found — check the ST-Link/J-Link/CMSIS-DAP is plugged in.
connect.rttHint: Streams RTT log output over SWD (needs rtt-target or SEGGER RTT in the firmware) and lets you watch global/static variables live — see the Watch panel below the log once connected.

monitor.clearConfirm: Clear the log buffer for this tab?
monitor.diffHexTitle: Highlight hex bytes that changed from the previous line
monitor.openLogFolderError: Could not open the log folder: {{message}}

dashboard.empty: No telemetry detected yet. This shows the latest value of any key=value, key: value, or flat-JSON fields the device sends.

dataInspector.title: Data inspector
dataInspector.invalidHex: Enter valid hex bytes (pairs of hex digits).
dataInspector.structTruncated: Ran out of bytes before the last field — decoded what fit.

colorBar.hint: Tint log lines matching a pattern. The first matching rule wins, and overrides the automatic level colour.

eventCounter.hint: Count how often a pattern appears in the buffer and how fast (matches in the last 10s).

globalSearch.title: Search all tabs
globalSearch.hint: Type to search across every open tab, not just the current one.
globalSearch.noMatches: No matching lines in any open tab.

broadcast.title: Broadcast send
broadcast.hint: Send one command to several open connections at once — useful on a production bench or a multi-node test.
broadcast.noOpenTabs: No open connections to send to.

logCompare.title: Compare logs
logCompare.placeholder: Paste log text, or load from an open tab
logCompare.truncated: Compared the last {{max}} lines of each side.

ota.hint: Flashes a device over WiFi using the ArduinoOTA protocol — the device must already be running firmware built with ArduinoOTA.h (Arduino/PlatformIO Arduino-framework projects). No serial port or USB cable involved.
ota.passwordPlaceholder: leave blank if the device has none

debug.hint: Reads a build's .elf directly (DWARF debug info + section headers) — no separate .map file and no bundled toolchain binary needed. Works the same for Xtensa or RISC-V ESP32 variants, or STM32/ARM.

crashDecoder.heading: Crash decoder
crashDecoder.placeholder:
Paste crash output here, e.g.:
Backtrace: 0x400d1e2d:0x3ffb9f30 0x400d1e97:0x3ffb9f50

coreDump.heading: Core dump decoder
coreDump.hint: Paste the base64 core dump the device prints on panic (CONFIG_ESP_COREDUMP_ENABLE_TO_UART), with or without the CORE DUMP START/END markers. Candidate addresses found in the dump are resolved against the .elf above — this is a heuristic scan, so expect some noise alongside the real frames.

productionStats.hint: Cross-session totals for devices flashed by a production workflow — ESP32 batch flash (including auto-flash-on-plug) and STM32 Mass Production. Ad-hoc single-device flashes during development aren't counted here.
productionStats.clearConfirm: Clear the whole production history? This cannot be undone.

ftp.title: FTP Server
ftp.serverHint: Runs a plain-FTP server on this computer that any device on your LAN can read/write files through — leave username blank to allow anonymous access. If nothing connects from another device, check that your firewall allows this app through (Windows may prompt for this the first time).
ftp.serverRunningHint: Serving on port {{port}}. Stop the server before changing settings.

tree.noFileOpen: No file open. Browse the sidebar and click a file to edit it.
tree.binaryFile: This file looks binary and can't be edited here.
tree.largeFileConfirm: "{{name}}" is {{size}} — opening it may be slow. Open anyway?
tree.closeTabUnsavedConfirm: This FTP tab has unsaved file changes. Close anyway and discard them?
tree.connectionLostWhileEditing: FTP connection lost — unsaved changes can't be saved until you reconnect.

flash.title: Flash
flash.smartAddTitle: Pick build output files and auto-fill offsets — reads the real partition table when you include one, instead of guessing
flash.eraseConfirm: Erase the entire chip? This cannot be undone.
flash.smartAddDialogTitle: Select ESP32 build files (bootloader, partitions, firmware, filesystem image…)
flash.partitionParseError: Could not parse partition table, offsets left blank where unsure: {{error}}
flash.unmatchedWarning: {{count}} file(s) couldn't be matched to a known offset — fill those in manually. This usually means the file didn't have a recognizable name, or its role was ambiguous (e.g. multiple app/OTA files without one you selected being an exact match).

mqtt.selectTopicPrompt: Select a topic to see its messages.
mqtt.clearRetainedTitle: Publish an empty retained message to clear it

plot.fftTitle: Frequency-domain view (FFT of the current buffer)
plot.mathTitle: Derived channels computed from existing ones
plot.levelsTitle: Horizontal alert levels — beep when a channel crosses one upward
plot.measureTitle: Measure Δt / Δy between two points (freezes the chart, click two points)
plot.measureHint: Click two points on the chart to measure the time and value between them.
plot.csvTitle: Export the whole buffer (including math channels) as CSV
plot.pngTitle: Export the chart as a PNG image (legend not included)
plot.extractorPatternPlaceholder: regex with one capture group, e.g. temp=(\d+\.\d+)
plot.mqttFieldsTitle: JSON fields picked from MQTT payloads to plot — add one from a topic's payload view
plot.mqttFieldsEmpty: No fields picked yet — open an MQTT tab's topic payload and click the chart icon on a numeric field.
plot.addMqttFieldTitle: Add this field to the plot
plot.removeMqttFieldTitle: Remove this field from the plot
plot.channelLimitReached: Plot already has the max {{max}} channels — stop whatever's producing the ones you don't need (e.g. a running plugin) and clear the chart before adding another.

macro.noStepsYet: No steps recorded yet — click Record, then send commands normally.

pluginBar.noneInstalled: No plugins installed yet — install one from the Plugins button in the top bar.

pluginLibrary.title: Plugins
pluginLibrary.hint: A plugin is a named, reusable Lua file that either decodes each line into fields (Decoder) or extracts numeric channels for the plotter (Plotter parser) — install one here, then enable it per tab from that tab's Plugins button.
pluginLibrary.deleteConfirm: Uninstall "{{name}}"? Any tab currently running it will stop.

modbus.disabledWhileSlaveListening: Disabled while the Modbus Slave emulator is listening on this tab
modbus.labelTitle: Label — also used as the plotter channel name
modbus.disabledWhilePollingActive: Disabled while Modbus poll rules are enabled on this tab

provision.autoRunLabel: Auto-run on plug (ESP32-like devices run this workflow immediately)
provision.noDevicesYet: No devices provisioned yet this session.

flashBatch.autoFlashLabel: Auto-flash on plug (ESP32-like devices flash immediately, no confirmation)
flashBatch.autoFlashNoSegments: Armed, but no firmware segments configured yet — nothing will flash until you add at least one.

stm32.massEraseConfirm: Mass erase the entire chip? This cannot be undone.
stm32.rdpWarning: WARNING: changing RDP (readout protection) can PERMANENTLY lock debug access to this chip if set incorrectly. This cannot always be undone.
stm32.cliNotFoundDetail: It cannot be bundled with EDT — ST's license does not permit redistributing it. Install STM32CubeProgrammer, then recheck below.
stm32.optionBytesAdvancedHint: Writes any option byte by name — for fields not covered by the curated Security section above (e.g. per-family Write Protection). Field names vary between STM32 families; check your MCU's reference manual.

writeMemory.title: Write memory
writeMemory.hint: Writes arbitrary content to a single address, independent of the firmware file above — the content is staged into a small temp file and flashed the same way a full firmware image would be.

security.rdpHint: Controls whether the chip's flash can be read out via SWD/JTAG or the bootloader. Read the current option bytes first to see the active level.
security.level0Desc: Flash can be freely read/written via SWD/JTAG. Default state for development.
security.level1Desc: Debug access and flash read are blocked until reverted to Level 0 (which mass-erases the chip). Reversible.
security.level2Desc: Permanently disables SWD/JTAG debug access. This CANNOT be undone or reverted to Level 0/1 — the chip can only ever be reflashed via its normal boot path.

massProduction.hint: Flashes the same firmware to many boards in a row, patching a unique value (serial number/MAC/key) into each copy first. Plug a board, click Flash & Provision, wait for it to finish, unplug, plug the next one, repeat — the counter advances automatically after each success.

swdWatch.name: Name
swdWatch.empty: No variables watched yet — load a .elf and pick one above.

quickCommands.lineEndingTitle: Line ending for this command (overrides the tab's setting)
quickCommands.wrapRowsTitle: Show all quick commands as wrapped rows instead of one scrolling line

signalBar.dtrTitle: Data Terminal Ready (writable — click to toggle)
signalBar.rtsAutoTitle: Controlled automatically by RS485 half-duplex mode
signalBar.rtsManualTitle: Request To Send (writable — click to toggle)

statsBar.gapTitle: Time between consecutive lines over the recent window: average (min–max)

triggerBar.webhookBodyTitle: Optional JSON body. Use {{line}} and {{pattern}} as placeholders; leave empty to send { line, pattern }.

send.checksumTitle: Appends a checksum to every sent message (suppresses the line ending above when active)
send.textPlaceholder: Type a command and press Enter

common.typeToConfirm: Type {{keyword}} to confirm — this cannot be undone.

security.enterPinPrompt: Enter the flash PIN to continue:

esp32Security.hint: Reads security-relevant eFuses (flash encryption, secure boot, JTAG/UART-download disable) directly from the chip — no espefuse.py/Python needed. Burning is irreversible; only the fields listed below (as returned by the chip's own eFuse layout) can be burned.
esp32Security.burnWarning: eFuses are one-time-programmable — burning is PERMANENT and cannot be undone. Setting the wrong value (or burning flash-encryption/secure-boot bits without also completing the required key setup) can render the device permanently unbootable. Read Espressif's flash encryption / secure boot documentation before using this.

settings.title: Settings

flashLock.title: Require PIN before flashing
flashLock.hint: Every flash action (ESP32/STM32 single flash, batch flash, mass production) will prompt for this PIN first — prevents a shared production station from being flashed by whoever walks up to it. This is stored in plain text on this machine; it's accidental/unauthorized-use prevention, not real access control.

backup.hint: Every saved library (connection profiles, scripts, plugins, quick-command profiles, filter/trigger presets) in one file — back this machine's setup up, or hand it to a teammate. Personal preferences (theme, language) and auto-tracked history aren't included.

settings.uiScaleHint: Scales the whole interface — text, icons, spacing — not just the monitor's log text.

restApi.title: Local REST API
restApi.hint: Lets an external CI/production-line system open a serial port, write/read bytes, and trigger an ESP32 flash on this running instance over HTTP. Binds to 127.0.0.1 only — never reachable from the LAN. Every request needs the token below in an "Authorization: Bearer <token>" header.
restApi.regenerateConfirm: Regenerate the token? Any external tool configured with the current one will need to be updated.
restApi.startError: Could not start the local REST API: {{error}}

onboarding.subtitle: One tool for serial, network, flashing and plotting — instead of three or four.
onboarding.monitorDesc: Multi-tab, hex/ASCII, filters, triggers, scripting — for serial, TCP/UDP, MQTT, WebSocket and SSH.
onboarding.flashDesc: Chip detect, multi-segment flash with progress, batch/OTA modes, crash decoder.
onboarding.plotDesc: Up to 8 channels with FFT, math channels, thresholds and measurement cursors.
onboarding.networkDesc: LAN scanner, FTP client/server, Modbus RTU/TCP — each with a fit-for-purpose view.
